threat_model: A terminal closure certificate could be mistaken for runtime promotion, runtime activation, or terminal closure authority if it permits authority flags or runtime execution claims. The mitigation is a schema-backed local-only certificate with hard false authority fields, zero remaining promotion evidence gates, and an explicit requirement for a separate runtime-promotion authorization action before activation.

required_checks.check: authority boundary check proves the certificate keeps runtime promotion, execution, dispatch, runtime replanning, success claims, terminal closure, and runtime execution false
promotion gate check proves the certificate records all promotion evidence gates as satisfied while runtime activation remains separately governed
certificate integrity check proves the checked-in fixture matches the deterministic produced certificate and validates against the schema
certificate-boundary check proves source witness binding, prior gate satisfaction, terminal certificate collection, runtime authorization requirement, and denied authority remain explicit

findings.mitigation: The schema and validator hard-deny runtime_promotion_authorized, execution_allowed, dispatch_allowed, runtime_replanning_enabled, success_claim_allowed, terminal_closure, and runtime execution fields.
The certificate separates evidence closure from runtime activation by requiring a separate runtime promotion authorization action before activation.

mitigations: keep the terminal closure certificate local, deterministic, and read-only
hard-deny all runtime promotion, execution, dispatch, runtime replanning, success, terminal closure, and runtime execution fields in the schema
bind the certificate to the deterministic replay/recovery witness id and hash
record zero remaining promotion evidence gates without claiming runtime activation
